questions.principle: Lollapalooza Tendency — The McDonnell Douglas Evacuation Test
questions.question: McDonnell Douglas ran a dangerous plane evacuation test twice on the same day, injuring dozens of people each time, including causing one case of permanent paralysis. How does MM's checklist analysis of this disaster illustrate the Lollapalooza Tendency?
questions.short_answer: At least six psychological tendencies acted simultaneously in the same direction: Reward-Super Response (urgency to pass the test), Doubt-Avoidance (commit to a plan and execute), Authority-Misinfluence (government's directive to be realistic), Inconsistency-Avoidance (plan already set), Social-Proof (no one objected), and Deprival-Super Reaction (can't lose the certification)—their confluence produced a result no single tendency could have caused.
questions.elaborated_answer: This case study is MM's favourite illustration of Lollapalooza Tendency in a non-experimental real-world setting. Individually, each tendency might have been overridden: someone might have questioned the test method despite Doubt-Avoidance, or spoken up despite Social-Proof. But six tendencies acting in confluence toward the same outcome—proceed with the dangerous test—was overwhelming. When the first test produced serious injuries, Deprival-Super Reaction and Inconsistency-Avoidance combined to suppress the obvious conclusion that the test method should be changed, and the company ran it again. The case also demonstrates the utility of the checklist approach: by going through the tendency list systematically, the analysis is immediate and complete; without the list, the result would appear as a puzzling corporate failure. This is MM's argument for why the checklist framework produces practical value that isolated experimental psychology cannot match.
questions.section: Talk 11: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment

questions.question: MM observes that cults have 'stumbled through practice evolution' into combining maximum psychological pressures on conversion targets. What does this evolutionary framing reveal about how dangerous social technologies develop?
questions.short_answer: It reveals that dangerous manipulative techniques do not require intentional design—through trial and error over time, the practices that most reliably produce extreme compliance survive and spread, meaning cults and predatory organisations can be sophisticated manipulators without any individual mastermind.
questions.elaborated_answer: The 'practice evolution' framing is important: MM is not claiming that cult founders consciously designed their induction weekends as psychological weapons. Rather, cults that happened to combine isolation, stress, social proof, authority, reciprocation, and commitment mechanisms into their recruitment processes retained more converts, grew larger, and were imitated by other groups. Through repeated cycles of variation and selection, effective confluence techniques accumulated in cult practice without any deliberate psychological engineering. This is why cults can be extraordinarily effective at a psychological level even when led by people with no formal psychology training: the selection pressure over many iterations produced optimised influence packages. The implication for anyone interacting with high-pressure organisations is that the absence of a visible manipulator is not evidence of the absence of manipulation—the manipulation may have accumulated through an evolutionary process that no individual designed.
questions.section: Talk 11: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment

questions.principle: Ten Practical Applications of Psychological Knowledge
questions.question: MM lists ten real-world examples of psychological knowledge being correctly applied—from Carl Braun's communication rules to the Constitutional Convention's design. What do all ten examples share as a structural feature?
questions.short_answer: All ten are procedural or structural interventions that change the decision-making environment rather than trying to change individual psychology directly—they work with human tendencies by redesigning context, not by exhorting people to overcome their nature.
questions.elaborated_answer: This structural similarity is central to MM's practical philosophy: psychological tendencies cannot simply be willed away, so the reliable approach is to design systems that either harness them constructively or prevent them from causing damage. Carl Braun's rule (require explanations) harnesses Reason-Respecting Tendency. Pilot simulators harness Use-It-or-Lose-It Tendency by maintaining skills that would otherwise atrophy. Alcoholics Anonymous harnesses multiple tendencies simultaneously, including Reciprocation, Social-Proof, and Inconsistency-Avoidance, to achieve what willpower alone cannot. The Constitutional Convention's secret-proceedings rule prevented Inconsistency-Avoidance Tendency from hardening delegates into locked positions before compromise was possible. Johnson & Johnson's acquisition autopsy process counterprogrammes the natural tendency to bury uncomfortable evidence. In each case, the insight is the same: design the process to make good behaviour psychologically natural and bad behaviour psychologically costly.
questions.section: Talk 11: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment

questions.principle: Ethics of Psychological Knowledge — The Paradox
questions.question: MM notes a paradox: the more people learn about Social-Proof Tendency, the less effective it becomes, which is precisely why the knowledge is valuable. What does this self-undermining quality reveal about the ethics of teaching psychology?
questions.short_answer: It means that teaching psychology widely is inherently constructive—knowledge of manipulation reduces its power—while keeping it secret primarily benefits manipulators who use it against the uninformed, making broad education the ethically correct as well as practically superior approach.
questions.elaborated_answer: In natural science, knowledge of gravity does not make gravity weaker. But in social psychology, knowledge that social proof is being used to influence you activates your critical faculties and reduces compliance. This creates a paradox: the science is most accurate when no one knows it, and least accurate when everyone does. The ethical implication is that psychological knowledge is inherently democratising: an informed population is harder to manipulate and makes better decisions collectively. The countervailing risk MM acknowledges—that the same knowledge improves manipulators' techniques—is real, but he argues that the benefits of broad knowledge outweigh the risks, because most people using knowledge defensively outnumber the manipulators who would use it offensively. Captain Cook's ethical use of psychology to get his sailors to eat sauerkraut (and avoid scurvy) illustrates the constructive case; the asymmetry between defence and offence in social psychology suggests that education primarily empowers defence.
